Bubbles in a Secular Stagnation Economy

An Abridged Model Replication

Author

Jason Lu

1 Introduction

Eggertsson & Mehrotra (2014) construct a three-period overlapping generations model in which a tightening of borrowing constraints permanently depresses the natural rate of interest below zero, trapping the economy at the zero lower bound. Lu (2015) extends this framework to show that a rational asset price bubble can restore the natural rate, raise output, and re-anchor inflation — but at the risk of economic instability.

2 Model

2.1 Households

Three generations coexist each period: young (\(y\)), middle-aged (\(m\)), and old (\(o\)). The young borrow up to a collateral limit \(D\) against future middle-aged income \(Y_m\). The middle-aged save. The old dissave. Market clearing in the loan market requires:

\[ D = \frac{\beta(Y_m - D)}{1 + r^f} \]

Solving for the natural rate:

\[ 1 + r^f = \frac{\beta(Y_m - D)}{D} \]

2.2 Bubble sustainability

A rational bubble of size \(A\) is sustainable if and only if it does not exceed the saving capacity of the middle-aged net of debt repayment:

\[ 0 \leq A \leq \bar{A}, \qquad \bar{A} = \frac{\beta Y_m - (1+2\beta)D}{1+\beta} \]

2.3 Production side

Output is determined by a standard New Keynesian AS/AD system. At the ZLB the nominal rate is fixed at \(i = 0\); the Taylor rule is suspended and inflation falls below target.

AS curve: \[ \pi_t = \kappa(Y_t - Y^f) + \beta \pi_{t+1} \]

AD curve (ZLB): \[ Y_t = Y_{t+1} - \sigma(i_t - \pi_{t+1} - r^n) \]

3 Secular Stagnation

3.1 The collateral shock

A tightening of the borrowing constraint from \(D_H\) to \(D_L\) permanently reduces the natural rate. With EM (2014) Table 1 parameters:

Natural rate at D_H = 0.28: r^f = 0.0727 (7.27%)
Natural rate at D_L = 0.259: r^f = -0.0358 (-3.58%)
ZLB binds at D_L: True

Proposition 1 (Eggertsson & Mehrotra 2014). If \(r^f(D_L) < 0\) the ZLB binds, monetary policy cannot restore full employment, and the economy settles at \(Y < Y^f\) with \(\pi < \pi^*\).

4 Bubble Restoration

4.1 Proposition 2 (Lu 2015)

A bubble \(A \in (0, \bar{A}]\) raises the natural rate by absorbing excess saving. At \(A = \bar{A}\) the natural rate is exactly zero and full employment is restored:

\[ Y\big|_{A=\bar{A}} = Y^f = 1, \qquad \pi\big|_{A=\bar{A}} = \pi^* \]

A_max at D_L = 0.259: 0.0634
A_max/2 = 0.0317

5 Stability and Policy

5.1 Critical shock thresholds

The bubble is fragile. A sufficiently large adverse shock to \(D\) or \(Y_m\) causes the bubble to violate the sustainability condition, forcing \(A \to 0\) and collapsing output back to the stagnation equilibrium.

6 Conclusion

Lu (2015) shows that rational bubbles are not merely destabilizing pathologies: in a secular stagnation economy they can serve as a coordination device that absorbs excess saving and restores full employment. The welfare case for tolerating or even engineering such bubbles depends critically on their stability, which is fragile to adverse shocks to borrowing constraints or income.

Replication by Jason Lu. All code available at https://github.com/jasonzhixinglu/bubble_secstag. Parameters from Eggertsson & Mehrotra (2014) Table 1.

7 References

Eggertsson, G. B. and Mehrotra, N. R. (2014). “A Model of Secular Stagnation.” NBER Working Paper 20574.

Lu, J. (2015). “Bubbles in a Secular Stagnation Economy.” University of Cambridge. First version: August 2015, last edited: March 2017.

Tirole, J. (1985). “Asset Bubbles and Overlapping Generations.” Econometrica, 53(5): 1071–1100.

Summers, L. (2013). “Why Stagnation Might Prove to Be the New Normal.” Financial Times, December 15.
